settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- agent-browser: Browser automation using Vercel's agent-browser CLI. Use when you need to interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, or scrape data. Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

`tailwind` is already effectively synced; `shadcn` is not.

- `packages/ui`:
  - `tailwindcss` is `catalog:` and resolves to `4.1.18` ([packages/ui/package.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/packages/ui/package.json#L97), [pnpm-workspace.yaml](/Users/duhl/git/ui/pnpm-workspace.yaml#L35), [pnpm-lock.yaml](/Users/duhl/git/ui/pnpm-lock.yaml#L1046))
  - Uses `tailwindcss-animate` (`^1.0.7`, resolved `1.0.7`) ([packages/ui/package.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/packages/ui/package.json#L84), [pnpm-lock.yaml](/Users/duhl/git/ui/pnpm-lock.yaml#L1046))
  - Uses shadcn config with `style: "default"` and shared tailwind config ([packages/ui/components.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/packages/ui/components.json#L12))
  - Uses classic per-package Radix deps (`@radix-ui/react-*`) ([packages/ui/package.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/packages/ui/package.json#L47))

- `apps/public-pages`:
  - `tailwindcss` is `catalog:` and resolves to `4.1.18` ([apps/public-pages/package.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/apps/public-pages/package.json#L54), [pnpm-lock.yaml](/Users/duhl/git/ui/pnpm-lock.yaml#L554))
  - Uses `tw-animate-css` (`^1.3.6`, resolved `1.4.0`) and imports it in CSS ([apps/public-pages/package.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/apps/public-pages/package.json#L55), [apps/public-pages/src/styles.css](/Users/duhl/git/ui/apps/public-pages/src/styles.css#L3), [pnpm-lock.yaml](/Users/duhl/git/ui/pnpm-lock.yaml#L557))
  - Uses shadcn config with `style: "new-york"` and app-local CSS ([apps/public-pages/components.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/apps/public-pages/components.json#L12))
  - Mixes `@radix-ui/react-*` with `radix-ui` meta package ([apps/public-pages/package.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/apps/public-pages/package.json#L24), [apps/public-pages/package.json](/Users/duhl/git/ui/apps/public-pages/package.json#L48))

- Important: there is no explicit pinned `shadcn` CLI/version in workspace manifests; only `components.json` schema/config is present.

Best low-risk sync plan for the whole monorepo:

1. Standardize dependency declarations first (no behavior change).
   - Move `@tailwindcss/postcss`, `@tailwindcss/vite`, `tailwind-merge`, and chosen animation package to `catalog` everywhere.
   - This removes drift in declared ranges while preserving current resolved versions.

2. Pick one shadcn baseline as repo standard.
   - Choose either:
     - `default + tailwindcss-animate + @radix-ui/react-*` (current `packages/ui` style), or
     - `new-york + tw-animate-css + radix-ui` (current `public-pages` style).
   - Don’t run mixed conventions long-term.

3. Migrate app-by-app behind visual regression checks.
   - For each app: align `components.json`, animation lib, and Radix import style.
   - Keep compatibility wrappers during transition so imports don’t break.

4. Make `@workspace/ui` the source of truth for shared shadcn components.
   - Stop generating duplicate base components in apps unless app-specific.
   - This is the biggest breakage reducer over time.

5. Enforce with CI.
   - Add a repo script/check that fails on non-catalog versions for Tailwind/shadcn-related deps and mixed animation libs.

If you want, I can implement step 1 now as a safe first PR (catalog-only alignment, no runtime changes).

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- agent-browser: Browser automation using Vercel's agent-browser CLI. Use when you need to interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, or scrape data. Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-browser/SKILL.md)

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- agent-browser: Browser automation using Vercel's agent-browser CLI. Use when you need to interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, or scrape data. Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-browser/SKILL.md)
- agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-native-architecture/SKILL.md)
- agent-native-audit: Run comprehensive agent-native architecture review with scored principles (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-native-audit/SKILL.md)
- agent-native-reviewer: Use this agent when reviewing code to ensure features are agent-native - that any action a user can take, an agent can also take, and anything a user can see, an agent can see. This enforces the principle that agents should have parity with users in capability and context. <example>Context: The user added a new feature to their application.\nuser: "I just implemented a new email filtering feature"\nassistant: "I'll use the agent-native-reviewer to verify this feature is accessible to agents"\n<commentary>New features need agent-native review to ensure agents can also filter emails, not just humans through UI.</commentary></example><example>Context: The user created a new UI workflow.\nuser: "I added a multi-step wizard for creating reports"\nassistant: "Let me check if this workflow is agent-native using the agent-native-reviewer"\n<commentary>UI workflows often miss agent accessibility - the reviewer checks for API/tool equivalents.</commentary></example> (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-native-reviewer/SKILL.md)

- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):

  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.

- Context hygiene:

  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". - Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". # Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.
